Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Regulation — transcriptional activator of both primary and secondary metabolic genes; reprograms carbon flux toward aromatic amino acid and phenylpropanoid biosynthesis
Genes.Effect_Direction: Overexpression increases flavonols and CGA to ~100 mg/g DW in fruit; increases aromatic amino acid content (except Phe); decreases major sugars; increases 14CO2 release from all glucose label positions indicating greater flux through glycolysis, OPPP, and TCA cycle
Genes.Evidence_Details: ChIP-qPCR: significant enrichment of ENO, DAHPS, PAL5A, PAL5C, PAL5D, CHS1, F3H promoters (P<0.05, Student's t-test, n=3); ChIP-seq confirms ENO, DAHPS, CHS1 peaks; RNA-seq: nearly all glycolysis, PPP, shikimate pathway genes upregulated; 14C-Glc feeding: significantly greater 14CO2 from C1, C3:C4, C6 positions (P<0.05); 13C-Glc: significantly higher label in shikimate/TCA products, reduced in sugars (Fig. 4b,c); conserved TACCTACC binding motif identified (Fig. 2d)
Genes.Evidence_Type: Genetic perturbation + metabolite quantification (LC-MS); RNA-seq transcriptomics; ChIP-qPCR and ChIP-seq (direct binding to ENO, DAHPS, PAL5A/C/D, CHS1, F3H promoters); 14C and 13C isotope feeding experiments showing increased carbon flux
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: Aromatic amino acids (Trp, Tyr increased significantly; Phe channeled into phenylpropanoids); major sugars decreased; organic acids and TCA cycle intermediates increased
Genes.Pathway: Glycolysis, oxidative pentose phosphate pathway, shikimate pathway, general phenylpropanoid pathway, flavonoid biosynthesis
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: Flavonols + CGA up to ~100 mg/g DW (10% fruit DW); in Indigo (AtMYB12 x Del/Ros1): CGA 2-fold more than AtMYB12 alone, flavonols 3-fold more, anthocyanins ~2-fold more than Del/Ros1; with VvStSy: resveratrol 5-6 mg/g DW (vs 0.5 mg/g DW without AtMYB12); with LjIFS + are-/-: isoflavones ~78 mg/g DW (vs 0.3 mg/g DW LjIFS alone)
Genes.Species: Arabidopsis thaliana (expressed in Solanum lycopersicum cv. MicroTom)
Genes.Tissue_Stage: Tomato fruit pericarp, ripe (7–10 days post breaker), driven by fruit-specific E8 promoter

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Regulation — endogenous tomato TF sharing target genes with AtMYB12
Genes.Effect_Direction: SlMYB12 expressed in peel correlates with elevated expression of ENO, DAHPS, CHS1, F3H, FLS in peel vs flesh
Genes.Evidence_Details: ChIP-qPCR scanning of ENO, DAHPS, CHS1 promoters in WT peel shows SlMYB12 binds same DNA motifs as AtMYB12 (P<0.05, Student's t-test, n=3; Fig. 3c); SlMYB12 protein detected by anti-MYB12 antibody at similar size to AtMYB12 (Supplementary Fig. 1)
Genes.Evidence_Type: ChIP-qPCR (direct binding to ENO, DAHPS, CHS1 promoters in WT peel); qRT-PCR expression correlation; immunodetection of protein
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: NA (inferred to be same as AtMYB12 targets)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Endogenous expression analysis (peel vs flesh comparison as natural perturbation); immunodetection and ChIP-qPCR
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: qRT-PCR: SlMYB12, SlENO, SlDAHPS, SlCHS1, SlF3H, SlFLS all significantly higher in peel than flesh (P<0.05 or P<0.01, n=3; Fig. 3b)
Genes.Tissue_Stage: Tomato fruit peel, 5 days after breaker (endogenous expression predominantly in peel, not flesh)

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Regulation — activates demand (late anthocyanin biosynthesis) without activating supply (primary metabolism)
Genes.Effect_Direction: Del/Ros1 activates late anthocyanin biosynthetic genes; does NOT upregulate primary metabolism or general phenylpropanoid genes (Fig. 5b); anthocyanin accumulation in purple fruit
Genes.Evidence_Details: RT-qPCR heat map (Fig. 5b) shows Del/Ros1 mainly activates late flavonoid genes; metabolite data in Fig. 5c and Supplementary Table 3; aromatic amino acid content not increased in Del/Ros1 fruit (Supplementary Fig. 3)
Genes.Evidence_Type: Genetic perturbation + metabolite quantification (LC-MS); RT-qPCR gene expression analysis
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: No significant increase in aromatic amino acids (Supplementary Fig. 3); does not activate primary metabolism genes
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Overexpression (Del/Ros1 transgenic tomato line); crossed with AtMYB12 to generate Indigo
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: In Indigo (Del/Ros1 x AtMYB12): anthocyanins ~2-fold higher than Del/Ros1 alone; CGA 2-fold more than AtMYB12; flavonols 3-fold more than AtMYB12 (Fig. 5c, P<0.05 or P<0.01)

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Regulation — activates demand (late anthocyanin biosynthesis) without activating supply
Genes.Effect_Direction: Same as Delila — activates late anthocyanin biosynthetic genes only
Genes.Evidence_Details: Co-expressed with Del; see Fig. 5b,c and Supplementary Table 3
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: No significant increase in aromatic amino acids
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Overexpression (Del/Ros1 transgenic tomato line); crossed with AtMYB12 to generate Indigo
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: Same as Del entry above; anthocyanins in Del/Ros1 fruit; ~2-fold increase in anthocyanins in Indigo vs Del/Ros1

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — catalyzes condensation of p-coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA to form resveratrol; acts as branch/flux controller diverting substrate away from CHS/flavonoid pathway
Genes.Effect_Direction: VvStSy alone: ~0.5 mg/g DW trans-resveratrol; VvStSy + AtMYB12: 5-6 mg/g DW total resveratrol compounds (10-12 fold increase); flavonols depleted in AtMYB12 StSy compared to AtMYB12 alone
Genes.Evidence_Details: LC-MS quantification in Fig. 6b,c and Supplementary Tables 4,5; comparison of VvStSy alone vs AtMYB12 x VvStSy genotypes selected from crosses
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: Flavonols reduced in AtMYB12 x StSy line (StSy competes effectively with CHS, redirecting flux from flavonoids to stilbenes)
Genes.Pathway: Stilbene biosynthesis (branch from general phenylpropanoid pathway; competes with CHS for p-coumaroyl-CoA)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Overexpression (35S:VvStSy transgenic tomato); crossed with E8:AtMYB12
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: VvStSy alone: ~0.5 mg/g DW; AtMYB12 + VvStSy: 5-6 mg/g DW total resveratrol (100-fold higher than red grapes); ~1 mg/g DW monomethylated resveratrol; 4-5 fold increase in polydatin; are-/- mutation did not significantly change resveratrol in AtMYB12 StSy (Supplementary Table 4)
Genes.Tissue_Stage: Tomato fruit, 10 days post breaker, driven by CaMV 35S promoter

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — catalyzes 2-hydroxylation of naringenin to form genistein (isoflavone); branch/flux controller competing with F3H for naringenin
Genes.Cellular_Compartment: NA (likely ER-associated, as a cytochrome P450)
Genes.Effect_Direction: LjIFS alone: ~0.3 mg/g DW genistin; LjIFS + AtMYB12: ~11 mg/g DW genistin; LjIFS + AtMYB12 + are-/-: ~78 mg/g DW total isoflavones
Genes.Evidence_Details: Multiple genotypes selected from F2 crosses; LC-MS quantification in Fig. 6d,e, Supplementary Figs 5,7 and Supplementary Table 5
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: Naringenin (substrate, shared with F3H); flavonols reduced when combined with are-/- mutation
Genes.Pathway: Isoflavone biosynthesis (branch from flavanone naringenin; competes with F3H for substrate)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Overexpression (E8:LjIFS transgenic tomato); crossed with AtMYB12 and are-/- mutant
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: LjIFS alone: ~0.3 mg/g DW genistin (2-10 fold higher than previous 35S:GmIFS reports); + AtMYB12: ~11 mg/g DW (~37-fold increase); + AtMYB12 + are-/-: ~78 mg/g DW (~260-fold increase from LjIFS alone; 100-fold higher than soy products like tofu/natto) (Fig. 6d,e; Supplementary Table 5)
Genes.Species: Lotus japonicus (expressed in Solanum lycopersicum cv. MicroTom)
Genes.Tissue_Stage: Tomato fruit, 10 days post breaker, driven by E8 promoter

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — key enzyme at branch point; loss of function redirects metabolic flux from flavonols toward isoflavones
Genes.Effect_Direction: are-/- reduces F3H activity → reduces flavonol production to ~30% of AtMYB12 level; redirects naringenin flux to IFS → dramatically increases isoflavone production
Genes.Evidence_Details: Crosses generating different genotype combinations; metabolite quantification in Fig. 6a,d and Supplementary Figs 6,7 and Supplementary Tables 4,5
Genes.Evidence_Type: Genetic perturbation (natural loss-of-function mutant) + metabolite quantification (LC-MS)
Genes.Intermediate_Metabolites_Affected: Naringenin (substrate accumulation redirected to IFS pathway)
Genes.Pathway: Flavonoid biosynthesis (converts naringenin to dihydrokaempferol; branch point with IFS)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Loss-of-function natural mutant (are-/-, anthocyanin reduced); combined with AtMYB12 and LjIFS
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: AtMYB12 are-/-: only 30% flavonols compared to AtMYB12 (Supplementary Fig. 6); AtMYB12 are-/- + LjIFS: ~78 mg/g DW isoflavones vs ~11 mg/g DW in AtMYB12 + LjIFS (Fig. 6d,e); are-/- did not significantly change resveratrol in AtMYB12 StSy background (Supplementary Table 4)

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — primary metabolism enzyme providing PEP precursor for shikimate pathway and aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Genes.Effect_Direction: Upregulated by AtMYB12; direct binding confirmed by ChIP-qPCR and ChIP-seq; contributes to increased carbon flux into shikimate pathway
Genes.Evidence_Details: ChIP-qPCR: significant binding of AtMYB12 around TACCTACC motif in ENO promoter (Fig. 3a); ChIP-seq peak at ENO promoter (Fig. 2c); SlMYB12 also binds ENO promoter in WT peel (Fig. 3c); expression elevated in peel vs flesh (Fig. 3b)
Genes.Evidence_Type: ChIP-qPCR (direct TF binding, P<0.05, n=3), ChIP-seq confirmation, RNA-seq upregulation, qRT-PCR expression data; isotope feeding shows increased glycolytic flux (indirect evidence for ENO contribution)
Final_Nutrient_Products: Phenylpropanoids (flavonols, CGA — indirectly via increased precursor supply)
Genes.Pathway: Glycolysis (converts 2-phosphoglycerate to phosphoenolpyruvate); links primary metabolism to shikimate pathway
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Transcriptional activation by AtMYB12/SlMYB12 (direct target; not independently knocked out/overexpressed in this study)
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: RNA-seq: significantly upregulated in AtMYB12 fruit; qRT-PCR: significantly higher expression in peel (where SlMYB12 is active) vs flesh (P<0.05, Fig. 3b)

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — rate-limiting entry enzyme of shikimate pathway; key determinant of carbon flow into aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Genes.Effect_Direction: Upregulated by AtMYB12; direct binding confirmed by ChIP-qPCR and ChIP-seq; key determinant of carbon flow into shikimate pathway
Genes.Evidence_Details: ChIP-qPCR: significant binding of AtMYB12 around TACCTACC motif in DAHPS promoter (Fig. 3a); ChIP-seq peak at DAHPS promoter (Fig. 2c); SlMYB12 also binds DAHPS promoter in WT peel (Fig. 3c); expression elevated in peel vs flesh (Fig. 3b)
Genes.Evidence_Type: ChIP-qPCR (direct TF binding, P<0.05, n=3), ChIP-seq confirmation, RNA-seq upregulation, qRT-PCR expression data
Final_Nutrient_Products: Phenylpropanoids (flavonols, CGA — indirectly via increased precursor supply)
Genes.Pathway: Shikimate pathway (first committed step; condenses PEP and erythrose-4-phosphate to DAHP)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Transcriptional activation by AtMYB12/SlMYB12 (direct target; not independently knocked out/overexpressed in this study)
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: RNA-seq: significantly upregulated in AtMYB12 fruit; qRT-PCR: significantly higher expression in peel vs flesh (P<0.05, Fig. 3b); 13C labeling shows significantly higher label in shikimate pathway products in AtMYB12 fruit (Fig. 4b)

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — first committed enzyme of flavonoid pathway; competes with StSy for p-coumaroyl-CoA
Genes.Effect_Direction: Upregulated by AtMYB12; direct binding confirmed by ChIP-qPCR and ChIP-seq
Genes.Evidence_Details: ChIP-qPCR scanning shows AtMYB12 binding around TACCTACC motif in CHS1 promoter (Fig. 3a); ChIP-seq peak (Fig. 2c); SlMYB12 binds same region in WT peel (Fig. 3c)
Genes.Evidence_Type: ChIP-qPCR (direct TF binding), ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, qRT-PCR
Genes.Pathway: Flavonoid biosynthesis (first committed step; condenses p-coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA to form naringenin chalcone)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Transcriptional activation by AtMYB12/SlMYB12 (direct target confirmed by ChIP)
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: RNA-seq: significantly upregulated; qRT-PCR: significantly higher in peel vs flesh (Fig. 3b); ChIP-qPCR: significant enrichment (P<0.05, n=3)

Genes.Biosynthesis_vs_Catabolism_vs_Transport_: Biosynthesis — gateway enzyme converting phenylalanine to phenylpropanoid metabolism
Genes.Effect_Direction: Upregulated by AtMYB12; direct binding confirmed
Genes.Evidence_Details: Fig. 2b: significant enrichment of PAL5A, PAL5C, PAL5D promoters in ChIPed DNA from AtMYB12 fruit
Final_Nutrient_Products: All phenylpropanoids (flavonols, CGA, anthocyanins, stilbenes, isoflavones)
Genes.Pathway: General phenylpropanoid pathway (first step; deaminates phenylalanine to trans-cinnamic acid)
Genes.Perturbation_Type: Transcriptional activation by AtMYB12 (direct targets confirmed by ChIP-qPCR)
Genes.Quantitative_Effect: ChIP-qPCR: promoters of PAL5A, PAL5C, PAL5D significantly enriched in AtMYB12 ChIP vs input (P<0.05, n=3; Fig. 2b)

Title: Multi-level engineering facilitates the production of phenylpropanoid compounds in tomato
